headline: How Spotify uses S3 Lifecycle Rules, Transfer Acceleration, and Server Access Logging
why: Spotify stores 100+ million tracks and podcast episodes in S3. Without lifecycle automation, the storage bill would be enormous. Without access logging, they couldn't prove GDPR compliance or debug CDN issues.

topics.usage: New podcast episodes go to S3 Standard. After 30 days, Lifecycle moves them to Standard-IA (most episodes get 80% of their plays in the first week). After 1 year, they move to Glacier. Old episodes from 2015 cost Spotify almost nothing to store.

Artists upload new tracks from anywhere in the world via Spotify for Artists. Transfer Acceleration routes uploads from São Paulo, Seoul, or Lagos through the nearest AWS edge to the master bucket in eu-west-1 — uploads complete in seconds not minutes.

Spotify logs every S3 access to a separate audit bucket. The logs show which IP downloaded which track at what time. Required for music licensing compliance — Spotify must be able to prove to record labels how many times a track was served.

diagram:
graph LR
    Artist([🎤 Artist Upload]) -->|Transfer Accel| Edge[AWS Edge Location]
    Edge --> Master[S3 Master Bucket\neu-west-1]
    Master --> Log[Access Log Bucket\nGDPR + Licensing audit]
    Master -->|Day 0–30| Standard[Standard\nNew releases]
    Standard -->|Day 30| IA[Standard-IA\nOlder episodes]
    IA -->|Day 365| Glacier[Glacier\n2015 podcasts]
    style Artist fill:#1e293b,color:#94a3b8
    style Edge fill:#7c3aed,color:#fff
    style Log fill:#450a0a,color:#fca5a5
    style Standard fill:#1e3a5f,color:#dbeafe
    style IA fill:#1a3320,color:#86efac
    style Glacier fill:#1a3320,color:#86efac
takeaway: Spotify's S3 setup is a textbook lifecycle pipeline: hot new content in Standard, older content auto-tiered to cheaper classes, every access logged for legal compliance. Three features, one bucket, massive cost savings.
